title: Beyond lead halide perovskites: visible light photovoltaics with phase engineered bismuth-based oxide double-perovskites, Bi2MCrO6 (M = Fe, Mn)
authors: N P Vikas, Ranjit K Pradhan, Somdutta Mukherjee, Udai P Singh, Biplab K Patra, Ravi P Srivastava, Amritendu Roy
abstract: Lead poisoning and notorious ambient instability in lead-based halide perovskites pave the way for the exploration of alternative materials for affordable and efficient solar cell fabrication. An important prerequisite to this end is the optoelectronic evaluation of the proposed material. Here we report, optoelectronic characterization of Bi2FeCrO6 (BFCO) and Bi2MnCrO6 (BMCO) thin films vis-à-vis performance of photovoltaic cells. Solution-deposited thin films (350-450 nm) of the above compositions demonstrate a double-perovskite structure with monoclinic P21/c symmetry, albeit with mixed cation valences and deep-level defects. A thorough optoelectronic evaluation exhibits large optical absorption in the visible range (α ~ 104 -105 cm-1), and high carrier density, ~1017-20 cm-3. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy measurement allowed determination of the positions of the band-edges (valence band maximum and conduction band minimum), required for the selection of carrier transport layers. In its first, BMCO-based FTO/SnO2/BMCO/Spiro-OMeTAD/Ag solar cell produced a maximum 3.56% conversion efficiency. Using numerical simulation, we predict that with suitable defect control, the above conversion efficiency can increase significantly.
summary: This paper investigates bismuth-based oxide double-perovskites as a non-toxic alternative to lead-based perovskites for solar cells. The researchers achieved visible-light absorption and demonstrated that while current efficiencies are modest, significant improvements are possible through defect engineering. The study provides a roadmap for optimizing these materials for efficient photovoltaics.
main_problem: Addressing the toxicity and instability of lead-halide perovskites by exploring bismuth-based oxide double-perovskites (Bi2FeCrO6 and Bi2MnCrO6) as stable, non-toxic alternatives for photovoltaics.
main_result: Demonstrated high optical absorption in the visible range and achieved power conversion efficiencies of 1.07% for BFCO and 3.56% for BMCO, with simulations predicting much higher efficiencies if defect density is controlled.
method: Solution-based deposition and thermal annealing were used for synthesis, followed by structural (XRD, SEM, AFM, EDS), electronic (XPS, UPS, Mott-Schottky), and optical (UV-Vis) characterization, alongside SCAPS-1D numerical device simulations.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of bismuth-based oxide double-perovskite thin films (Bi2FeCrO6 and Bi2MnCrO6) with monoclinic P21/c symmetry, integrated into a solar cell architecture: FTO/SnO2/Absorber/Spiro-OMeTAD/Ag.
key_observables: Optical absorption coefficient, bandgap energy, band-edge positions (VBM/CBM), carrier density, power conversion efficiency (PCE), and Urbach energy.
important_parameters: Absorption coefficient (10^4-10^5 cm^-1), carrier density (10^17-10^20 cm^-3), and defect density (Nt).
main_assumptions: The numerical simulations assume that reducing defect density to approximately 10^13 cm^-3 can significantly boost efficiency.
figures_summary: The notes mention a schematic of the device architecture (Fig. 5b).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of lead toxicity, describes the synthesis and structural characterization of the bismuth-based oxides, presents optoelectronic and device performance results, and concludes with numerical simulations predicting future optimization potential.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
